1: nega echad od: 'one plague more' — literally 'one stroke/blow still.' The word nega (H5061, plague, blow, stroke) is used throughout Leviticus for skin diseases but here describes the final divine blow. The phrase acharei ken ('after that') signals the definitive end: after this plague Pharaoh will not merely let Israel go but drive them out (garash) — the expulsion will be total and urgent. YHWH's control over the timing ('I will bring') is emphasized: the final plague waits on YHWH's initiative, not Pharaoh's decision.
2: veyishalu ish meyit reyahu veisha meyit reutah keli-keseph u-chelei zahav: 'each man shall ask of his neighbor and each woman of her neighbor articles of silver and gold.' The verb sha'al (H7592, to ask, borrow, request) is used — the same verb behind the Sheol wordplay in OT (sheol = place of asking/seeking?) and behind Samuel's name. Israel is instructed to ask/borrow the Egyptians' valuables. Exodus 12:36 will describe the fulfillment: they “plundered” (vayenatzlu, Piel of natsal, to strip/plunder) Egypt. The plundering of Egypt was the original fulfillment of YHWH's promise to Abraham that Israel would leave with 'great possessions' (Genesis 15:14).
3: vayiten YHWH et-chen ha'am beynei Mitzrayim: 'YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of the Egyptians.' The noun chen (H2580, favor, grace, charm) — YHWH sovereignly disposed the Egyptians to view the Israelites favorably, so that they would willingly give their valuables. The same chen vocabulary describes Joseph's favor in Egypt (Genesis 39:4), Moses himself before Pharaoh (Exodus 11:3: gadol meod beynei avdei pharoh u-veynei ha'am), and ultimately God's favor on believers in the NT (Luke 1:30; Acts 2:47). Divine favor is not earned by the recipient but given by YHWH to accomplish his purposes.
4: kachatzot halaylah: 'about midnight' — the time of maximum darkness and vulnerability. The phrase kachatzot uses the preposition ke- (like, approximately) — either “about midnight” or a divine approximation. Rabbinic tradition debated whether this was exactly midnight (YHWH's clock is precise) or approximately (human calculation is imprecise). The midnight setting is theologically rich: the darkest hour of the darkest plague is YHWH's appointed time. The Passover night's midnight becomes the paradigm for the eschatological night-watch: 'the bridegroom comes at midnight' (Matthew 25:6).
5: kol bekhor beeretz mitzrayim yamut: 'every firstborn in Egypt shall die.' The scope is total: from bekhor par'oh hayoshev al-kis'o (the firstborn of Pharaoh on his throne) to bekhor hashifchah asher achar harechayim (the firstborn of the slave-girl behind the millstones) to the firstborn of animals. The tenth plague targets the bekhor (firstborn, H1060) — the one who bears the legal primogeniture rights and the one who carries the family line. Egypt's future, from throne to stable, is struck. The logic: Egypt enslaved Israel, YHWH's firstborn son (Exodus 4:22-23: “Israel is my son, my firstborn; let my son go”), so YHWH strikes Egypt's firstborn.
6: vehayetah tzeaqah gedolah bechol-eretz mitzrayim: 'there will be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt.' The word tzeaqah (H6818, cry, outcry) is used for the cry of the oppressed: it is the tzeaqat Sodom that rose to YHWH (Genesis 18:20), and it is the cry of Israel under slavery that YHWH heard (Exodus 3:7: vaeshma et-tzaakatam). The same cry Israel made under oppression is now reversed and falls on Egypt. The tzeaqah motif in the OT establishes that YHWH hears the cry of the oppressed — and his justice eventually turns oppressor and oppressed.
7: ul'chol benei Yisrael lo yecheratz kelev leshono: 'but to all the Israelites not even a dog shall move its tongue.' The idiom lo yecheratz kelev leshono ('no dog will sharpen/whet its tongue') describes complete silence and calm among Israel during the plague. The contrast is stark: Egypt's tzeaqah gedolah (great cry) vs. Israel's silence. Yafle YHWH ('YHWH will work wonders/distinctions') — the verb palah (H6395, to distinguish, separate, make wonderful) is the key term for YHWH's covenant distinction between Israel and Egypt throughout the plagues. The same distinction that protected Israel from the plagues now preserves them through the final judgment.
8: veyardu kol-avadecha eleh elai vehishtachavu-li: 'and all these servants of yours will come down to me and bow down to me.' Moses's prophecy is precise: Pharaoh himself will not bow, but his officials will come begging. Bechemah gedolah ('in great anger') — Moses's departure from Pharaoh's presence in great anger (af, H639, anger, nose) is the only place in the plague narrative where Moses's emotional state is described. The prophet's righteous anger at Pharaoh's persistent hardness mirrors divine anger (cf. Mark 3:5: Jesus looked at the Pharisees with anger at their hardness of heart).
9: lema'an revot moftai beEretz Mitzrayim: 'in order that my wonders may be multiplied in Egypt.' The purpose (lema'an) clause here provides the theological framework for the entire plague cycle: the plagues are not only punitive but revelatory. The multiplication (revot, Qal infinitive of ravah) of wonders serves divine self-disclosure. Moftim (H4159, wonders, signs) is paired with otot (signs) throughout the Exodus narrative (Exodus 7:3; Exodus 11:9-10) to describe the plagues as the definitive divine testimony to YHWH's incomparable power. The NT uses the same semeia kai terata (signs and wonders) vocabulary for Jesus's miracles and the apostolic ministry (Acts 2:43; Exodus 5:12; Hebrews 2:4).
10: vaMoshe veAharon asu et-kol-hamoftim haeleh lifnei par'oh: 'Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh.' This verse summarizes the entire plague narrative (Exodus 7-11) before the Passover account. The repeated summary also marks the transition: the moftim performed before Pharaoh are now complete; what follows is the pivotal final act. Vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev par'oh — the final hardening before the final plague. The summary formula has appeared ten times through the plague narrative; this last occurrence closes the loop and prepares for the Passover night.
